lines.greek: τὸν δʼ αὖτε προσέειπε θεά, γλαυκῶπις Ἀθήνη·
οὐ μέν τοι γενεήν γε θεοὶ νώνυμνον ὀπίσσω
θῆκαν, ἐπεὶ σέ γε τοῖον ἐγείνατο Πηνελόπεια.
ἀλλʼ ἄγε μοι τόδε εἰπὲ καὶ ἀτρεκέως κατάλεξον·
τίς δαίς, τίς δὲ ὅμιλος ὅδʼ ἔπλετο; τίπτε δέ σε χρεώ;
εἰλαπίνη ἠὲ γάμος; ἐπεὶ οὐκ ἔρανος τάδε γʼ ἐστίν·
ὥς τέ μοι ὑβρίζοντες ὑπερφιάλως δοκέουσι
δαίνυσθαι κατὰ δῶμα. νεμεσσήσαιτό κεν ἀνὴρ
αἴσχεα πόλλʼ ὁρόων, ὅς τις πινυτός γε μετέλθοι.
τὴν δʼ αὖ Τηλέμαχος πεπνυμένος ἀντίον ηὔδα·
ξεῖνʼ, ἐπεὶ ἂρ δὴ ταῦτά μʼ ἀνείρεαι ἠδὲ μεταλλᾷς,
μέλλεν μέν ποτε οἶκος ὅδʼ ἀφνειὸς καὶ ἀμύμων
ἔμμεναι, ὄφρʼ ἔτι κεῖνος ἀνὴρ ἐπιδήμιος ἦεν·
νῦν δʼ ἑτέρως ἐβόλοντο θεοὶ κακὰ μητιόωντες,
οἳ κεῖνον μὲν ἄιστον ἐποίησαν περὶ πάντων
ἀνθρώπων, ἐπεὶ οὔ κε θανόντι περ ὧδʼ ἀκαχοίμην,
εἰ μετὰ οἷς ἑτάροισι δάμη Τρώων ἐνὶ δήμῳ,
ἠὲ φίλων ἐν χερσίν, ἐπεὶ πόλεμον τολύπευσεν.
τῷ κέν οἱ τύμβον μὲν ἐποίησαν Παναχαιοί,
ἠδέ κε καὶ ᾧ παιδὶ μέγα κλέος ἤρατʼ ὀπίσσω.

interlinear: and-him in-turn addressed the-goddess, bright-eyed Athena:
'not indeed for-you a-lineage nameless hereafter
have-the-gods appointed, since you such-a-one bore Penelope.
but come, to-me this tell, and truly recount:
what feast, what gathering is-this? and-what need have-you of-it?
a-banquet or a-wedding? since not a-shared-meal this at-least is;
so to-me insolently, overbearingly, they-seem
to-feast throughout the-house. indignant would-be a-man,
shameful-things many seeing, whoever prudent should-come-among-them.'
and-her in-turn Telemachus, prudent, answering addressed:
'Stranger, since indeed these-things you-ask-me and inquire,
it-was-destined once this-house wealthy and blameless
to-be, while still that man at-home was;
but-now otherwise have-willed the-gods, devising evils,
who that-man unseen have-made beyond all
men; since not for-him dead would-I-so-grieve,
if among his-comrades he-had-been-slain in-the-land of-the-Trojans,
or in-the-hands of-friends, when he-had-wound-up the-war.
then for-him a-tomb would-have-made all-the-Achaeans,
and-also for-his son great renown he-would-have-won hereafter.
literal: Then the goddess, bright-eyed Athena, answered him:
'Surely the gods have not appointed your line to be nameless
hereafter, since Penelope bore such a son as you.
But come, tell me this, and recount it truly:
what feast is this, what gathering? What need have you of it?
A banquet, or a wedding? For this is surely no shared meal;
so insolently and overbearingly do they seem to me
to feast throughout the house. A man would be indignant
to see so many shameful things, if he came among them with sense.'
Then prudent Telemachus answered her:
'Stranger, since you ask me this and inquire of it,
this house was once destined to be wealthy and blameless,
while that man was still at home;
but now the gods have willed otherwise, devising evils,
who have made him vanish beyond all
men. For I would not grieve so for him dead,
if he had been slain among his comrades in the land of the Trojans,
or in the hands of his friends, when he had wound up the war.
Then all the Achaeans would have made him a tomb,
and he would have won great renown for his son hereafter.
translations.elizabethan:
Then spoke the goddess, bright of eye, again:
"Surely the gods have not ordained thy line
to want a name hereafter, seeing that
Penelope bore such a son as thou.
But tell me true: what feast is this, what throng?
What need hast thou of them? A banquet? Or
a wedding? For no shared repast is here.
So insolent and overweening seem
these men that feast throughout thy hall. A man
of any sense, come newly in, would rage
to look on shame so manifold."
Then answered prudent Telemachus:
"Stranger, since thou dost ask and search it out,
this house was rich and blameless once, and would
have so remained, while yet that man was home.
But now the gods have willed it otherwise,
devising evil, and have made him vanish
past all men living. I should grieve him less
were he but dead, cut down among his men
in Trojan country, or in friendly arms
when he had wound the war up to its end.
Then had the Achaeans raised a mound for him,
and he had won his son great fame to come."
translations.modernist:
Then bright-eyed Athena spoke to him again.

"The gods have not left your line without a name. Not with Penelope bearing a son like you.

But tell me this, and tell it straight. What is this feast? What is this crowd? What do you want with them? Is it a banquet or a wedding? Nobody brought his own food to this one. They eat their way through your house and they do it like men who own it. Anyone with sense who walked in here would be angry at what he saw."

Then Telemachus answered her. He had sense.

"Stranger, since you ask, and since you want to know. This house was rich once, and it was respectable, while that man was still at home. The gods wanted it another way. They have made him disappear more completely than any man. I could stand it if he were dead. If he had gone down with his men in Trojan country, or died in his friends' arms with the war finished. The Achaeans would have built him a mound. He would have left his son a great name."
translations.storybook:
Then bright-eyed Athena spoke to him again.

"The gods have certainly not left your family without a name, not when Penelope has a son like you!

But do tell me: what is this feast? Who are all these people, and what do you want with them? Is it a party, or a wedding? Because nobody here has brought any food of his own! They are eating their way through your house as if they owned it, and behaving dreadfully. Anyone sensible who walked in would be furious at the sight."

Then sensible young Telemachus answered her.

"Stranger, since you ask me, I will tell you. This house was rich once, and respectable too, back when my father was still at home. But the gods decided otherwise, and they have made him vanish more completely than anybody in the world. I could bear it if he were simply dead. If he had fallen among his men at Troy, or died in the arms of his friends once the fighting was over, then all the Achaeans would have built him a great burial mound, and he would have left me a proud name to carry."

murray.text: What feast, what throng is this? What need hast thou of it? Is it a drinking bout, or a wedding feast? For this plainly is no meal to which each brings his portion, with such outrage and overweening do they seem to me to be feasting in thy halls. Angered would a man be at seeing all these shameful acts, any man of sense who should come among them.”

Then wise Telemachus answered her: “Stranger, since indeed thou dost ask and question me of this, our house once bade fair to be rich and honorable, so long as that man was still among his people. But now the gods have willed otherwise in their evil devising,

seeing that they have caused him to pass from sight as they have no other man. For I should not so grieve for his death, if he had been slain among his comrades in the land of the Trojans, or had died in the arms of his friends, when he had wound up the skein of war. Then would the whole host of the Achaeans have made him a tomb,

and for his son, too, he would have won great glory in days to come. But as it is, the spirits of the storm have swept him away and left no tidings: he is gone out of sight, out of hearing, and for me he has left anguish and weeping; nor do I in any wise mourn and wail for him alone, seeing that the gods have brought upon me other sore troubles.
